title: 第 15 節
en: These childish ideas, however, this simple wonder- AGE OF THE VEDA. 108 ment at the commonest events in nature, soon led on to more exalted ideas. One poet asks (Rv. X.
' How many fires are there, how many suns, how many dawns, and how many waters ? I do not say this, O fathers, to worry you ; I ask you, 0 seers, that I may know it.'
Another says :
' What was the wood, and what was the tree from which they have cut out heaven and earth ? '
(Rv. X. 31, 7 ; 81, 4.) Kim svit vanam kah u sa/i vriksha/i asa yata/i dj avapy^'thivi ni/i-tatakshii/i.
Or again, X. 81, 2:
' What was the stand on which he rested, which was it and how, from whence the All-maker, the allseeing, created the earth and spread out the sky by his might?'
Kim svit asit adhish^/^anam arambhai?am katamat svit katha asit, yata/^, bhumim ^(/anayan vi6vakarma vi dyam aiii-not mahina vi»va/i;aksha/i.

We see here how difficult it would be to draw a line between what we call childishness and what we call wisdom from the mouths of babes. If it is true that il ny a quun pas du sublime au ridicule, it would seem to be equally true that il i^'y a quun pas du ridicule au sublime. A childish question may call forth an answer full of profound wisdom. But to say that we look in vain for simple and primitive thoughts in the Veda is to set up a standard of simplicity and primitiveness that w^ould apply to cave-dwellers rather and prehistoric monsters, and not to people who, as long as we know them, were in full possession of one of the most perfect of Aryan 104 LECTURE V.

lanffuao-es. No doubt there are in the Veda thoughts and sentiments also that might have been uttered in the nineteenth century. But this only serves to show how large a period is covered by those ancient hymns, and how many different minds are reflected in it.
The Sacrificial Character of the Vedic Hymns.

Another view of the Veda, first advanced by Professor Ludwig, has of late been defended with great ingenuity by a French scholar, M. Bergaigne, a man whose death has been a serious loss to our studies. He held that all, or nearly all the Vedic hymns, were modern, artificial and chiefly composed for the sake of the sacrifice. Other scholars have followed his lead, till at last it has almost become a new doctrine that everywhere in the world sacrifice preceded sacred poetry. Here again we find truth and untruth strangely mixed together.

It is well known that in several cases verses contained in hymns, totally unconnected with the sacrifice, were slightly changed in order to adapt them to the requirements of the sacrificial ceremonial. The first verse, for instance, of the dialogue between Yama and Yami (Rv. .X. 10, 1), is 6 kit sakhayam sakhya vavr/ty&m, 'May I bring near the friend by friendship.'
In the Sama-veda, X. 340, the same verse appears as a tva sakhaya/« sakhya xaxrityuh, ' May the friends bring thee near by friendship/ that is, 'May the priests bring the god to the sacrifice ^.'

1 Von Schroeder, Indiens Literaiur, p. ]68; A past. Paribh Sutra AGE OF THE VEDA. 1U5 That many Vedic hymny, however, contain allusions to what may be called sacriticial customs, no one who has ever looked into the Veda can deny. Some of the hymns, and generally those which for other reasons also would be treated as comparatively late, presuppose what we should call a highly developed system of sacrificial technicalities. The distinction, for instance, between a verse {rlk), and a song (saman), and a sacrificial formula (ya^us), the distinction on which, as we saw, rests the division of the Veda into Rig-veda, Sama-veda, and Ya(/ur-veda. is found in one of the hymns, X. 90, and there only. But curiously enough, this very hymn is one of those that occur at the end of an Anuvaka, and contains several other indications of its relatively modern character. Many similar passages, full of sacrificial technicalities, have been pointed out ^ in the Rig-veda, and they certainly show that when these passages were composed, the sacrifice in India had already assumed what seems to us a very advanced, or, if you like, a very degraded and artificial character.

But there are other passages also where the poet says, ' Whosoever sacrifices to Agni with a stick of wood, with a libation, w^ith a bundle of herbs, or with an inclination of his head,' he will be blessed with many blessings (Rv. VIII. 19, 5 ; 10'?, 19).

This whole question, so hotly discussed of late, whether sacrifice comes first or prayer, whether the Vedic poets waited till the ceremonial was fully ^ The most complete collection of sacrificial terms occurring in the hymns of the Rig-veda may be found in Ludwig's Die ManfraUtferatur, 1878. pp. 35-3-415. Bergaigne's Religion Vedique appeared from 1878 to 1883.

developed before they invoked the Dawn, and the Sun, and the Storms to bless them, or whether, on the contrary, their spontaneous prayers suggested the performance of sacrificial acts, repeated at certain times of the day, of the month, of the year, is impossible to solve, because, as it seems to me, it is 4i\rrongly put.

' Sacrifice,' as Grimm remarked long ago, ' is only a prayer ofi'ered with gifts.' We nowhere hear of a mute sacrifice. What we call sacrifice, the ancients called simply karma, an act. Now in one sense a simple prayer, preceded by a washing of the hands, or accompanied by an inclination of the head, may be called a karma, an act^. On the other hand, a man who in lighting the fire on the hearth or in putting one log on the smouldering ashes, bows his head (namas)^ raises his arms (uttanahasta/i^ Rv. VI. 16, 46), and utters the name of Agni with some kind epithets (ya^/us), may be said to have addressed a hymn of praise to the god of fire. Prayer and sacrifice may have been originally inseparable, but in human nature I should say that prayer comes always fii'st, sacrifice second.

That the idea of sacrifice did not exist at a very early period, we may gather from the fact that in the common dictionary of the Aryan nations there is no word for it, while Sanskrit and Zend have not only the same name for sacrifice, but share together a great many words which refer to minute technicalities of the ancient ceremonial.
^ Kalpa, act, in the plural, occurs Rv. IX. 9, 7.
AGE OF THE VEDA. 107 Yag, to sacrifice.

The usual word for sacrificing in Sanskrit is YAG, Zend yaz, from which ya(/;la, sacrifice, yag-us, sacrificial formula, ya^ami, I sacrifice, ya^ya, to be worshipped. This ya^ya has been compared with Greek aytoj, sacred, though this is not certain ^ Why ya^ should have taken that meaning of sacrificing, or giving to the gods, we cannot tell ^, for it is impossible to trace that root back to any other root of a more general meaning.
Hu, to sacrifice.

Another Sanskrit root which has frequently to be translated by sacrificing is HU, In this case we can clearly see the original intention of the root. It meant to pour out, and was chiefly applied to the act of throwing barley and oil and other substances into the fii'e -K It afterwards took a more general meaning, not so general, however, as to be applicable to animal sacrifices. From it we have in Sanskrit ha vis, havya, sacrifice, a-hava, a jug, gruhu, a spoon, ho-t7'^, priest, homa and ahuti, libation. In Greek Xy or xeF means simply to pour out, yv-rpa, an earthenware pot*, (dveiv, to sacrifice, might phonetically ' The Greek d-yos or ayos does not mean sacrifice, but rather expiation. It cannot be the Sk. agas, because in Greek the a is short.

2 Sanskrit theologians connect yar/ with iysig, to give up, to leave, but there is no analogy for this. Comparative philologists used to place bha^, to worship, by the side of yag, assigning to bh (bhi) and y (ni or ti; a prepositional origin, but this is a pure hypothesis, which has long been surrendered.
3 Al-Biruni, ii. p. 96.

^ Aufrecht in Kuhn's Zeitschrift, xiv. p. 2C8. This root hu, to pour out, exists also in the Latin fuHs, a water-jug, and in vasa futiU'a, which Paulus, Ejnt. p. 89, explains rightly as derived a fimdendo. Ftdilis, in the sense of futile, may have been conceived either as a man who always pours forth, or as a vessel, leaky, not holding 108 LECTURE V.
be traced back to the same source, but its meanings cause difficulty.
Sacrificial Terms.
zh: 這些幼稚的觀念，這種對自然界最平凡事件的單純驚歎，很快就導向了更高尚的思想。一位詩人提出疑問（梨俱吠陀 X.
「有多少火焰，有多少太陽，有多少曙光，有多少水？我說這些，父輩啊，並非要擾亂你們；我問你們，先見者啊，好讓我能夠明白。」
另有人說：
「他們用什麼木材和什麼樹木切割出了天地？」
（梨俱吠陀 X. 31, 7；81, 4）Kim svit vanam kah u sa/i vriksha/i asa yata/i dj avapy^'thivi ni/i-tatakshii/i.
或再次如梨俱吠陀 X. 81, 2：
「他所憑藉的立足點是什麼，它是什麼以及如何存在？萬物之造者、遍觀一切者，從何處創造大地，以其神力展開天空？」
我注意到您提供的文本並非標準英文，而是梵文音譯（可能是《梨俱吠陀》的經文）。根據您的指示，我只應翻譯英文原文。 請提供英文版本的原文，我將按照您的規則進行繁體中文翻譯。

我們在此看見，在所謂的幼稚與所謂的智慧之間劃一條界線會是多麼困難——尤其當這些話語出自嬰孩之口時。若所謂「從崇高到荒謬只有一步」（il n'y a qu'un pas du sublime au ridicule）是真的，那麼同樣似乎也可以說「從荒謬到崇高也只有一步」（il n'y a qu'un pas du ridicule au sublime）。一個幼稚的問題可能引發充滿深刻智慧的答案。但要說我們在《吠陀》中枉然尋求簡樸與原始的思想，這是設定了一種簡樸與原始性的標準——這種標準只適用於洞窟居民和史前怪物，而不適用於那些始終以來就完全掌握了雅利安語族最完美語言之一的民族。
語言的疾病。誠然，《吠陀》中確實存在著某些思想和情感，亦可能出現於十九世紀。但這僅足以說明那些古老讚歌所涵蓋的時代跨度之廣闊，以及其中所反映的思想心靈之眾多。
吠陀讚歌的祭祀特質
關於吠陀的另一種觀點，最初由路德維希教授提出，近來由一位法國學者貝爾蓋涅先生以高超的巧思為其辯護，此人之逝世對我們的學術研究實為重大損失。他主張所有或幾乎所有的吠陀讚歌都是後出的、人工的，且主要是為了祭祀的目的而作成。其他學者紛紛跟隨他的論點，直至最後幾乎形成了一項新的教說：世界各地無不皆是祭祀先於聖詩。在此我們再次發現真理與謬誤如何奇異地混雜在一起。
眾所周知，在多個案例中，讚美詩所包含的詩句原本與祭祀毫無關聯，卻被略微改動以適應祭祀儀式的要求。例如，亞瑪與亞彌（Yama and Yami）之間對話的第一句（梨俱吠陀 X. 10, 1），即「kit sakhayam sakhya vavr/ty&m」，意為「願我以友誼將友人招致身邊」。
在《薩摩吠陀》X. 340 中，同一詩句出現為「tvá sakhayā sakhyà mártiyuh」，意為「願朋友以友誼將汝帶近」，亦即「願祭司以友誼將神帶往祭祀」。

馮‧施羅德《印度文學》第一六八頁；《往事‧帕里婆克經》(Apasst. Paribh Sutra)。**吠陀時代** 一〇五 誠然，許多吠陀讚歌確含有可稱之為祭祀習俗的隱喻，凡是讀過吠陀者無不承認此點。某些讚歌，尤其那些基於其他理由而被視為相對較晚的讚歌，預設了一套我們所謂高度發展的祭祀技法制度。例如，詩句（rik）與歌曲（saman）及祭祀頌詞（yajus）之間的區分——如我們所見，正是以此區分為基礎而將吠陀分為梨俱吠陀、娑摩吠陀及耶柱吠陀——這種區分僅見於一部讚歌，即第十卷第九十讚（X. 90），別無他處。然而饒有趣味的是，這部讚歌恰好正是出現在某個《阿紐瓦卡》(Anuvaka)末尾的讚歌，且包含若干其他足以表明其相對現代特質的跡象。許多類似的篇章，充滿祭祀技法細節，已在梨俱吠陀中被指出，而且無疑表明，當這些篇章被創作時，印度的祭祀已經呈現出對我們而言極為發達的，或者倘若你願意這麼說，極為退化與人工的特徵。
但在其他段落中，詩人也說過：「凡以木棒、澆奠、一束香草或俯首低眉向阿格尼祭祀者」，將蒙受眾多祝福（梨俱吠陀 VIII. 19, 5；10?, 19）。
這整個問題——近來引起熱烈討論——究竟是祭祀先於禱告，還是吠陀詩人要等到儀式完全^ 梨俱吠陀讚歌中出現的最完整的祭祀用語彙編可見於路德維希（Ludwig）的《曼夫拉文獻》（*Die Manfraliteratur*），1878年，第35-314頁。貝爾蓋涅（Bergaigne）的《吠陀宗教》（*Religion Vedique*）出版於1878至1883年。
在他們祈求曙光、太陽和風暴降福於他們之前，這些祭祀儀式是否已然發展完成，或者相反地，他們自發的祈禱是否反而促使祭祀行為的產生——這些行為在一日、一月、一年的特定時刻重覆進行——要解決這個問題是不可能的，因為在我看來，這個問題本身就被錯誤地設定了。

「祭祀」如格林（Grimm）早年所論，「不過是伴隨禮物獻上的祈禱」。我們無處聽聞無言的祭祀。古人所謂祭祀，簡稱為業（karma），即一種行為。在某種意義上，一種簡單的祈禱，先於洗手之舉，或伴以頷首之禮，可以稱為業（karma），一種行為^。另一方面，一個人在生火爐或將一根木柴放在悶燒的灰燼上時，低頭行禮（namas）^、舉起雙臂（uttanahasta）（梨俱吠陀 VI. 16, 46）、誦念火神阿耆尼（Agni）的名號並加以頌讚之詞（ya^us），可以說是已向火神奉上了讚美詩。祈禱與祭祀原本可能不可分，但就人類本性而言，我認為祈禱始終在先，祭祀次之。
從雅利安民族的共同詞彙中沒有「祭祀」一詞這一事實，我們可以推斷祭祀的觀念在很早的時期並不存在；相比之下，梵文和阿維斯陀語不僅對祭祀共享同一名稱，而且還共同保有大量與古代儀式繁瑣技術細節相關的詞彙。
^ Kalpa（劫）一詞，作為「行為」的含義，其複數形式出現於《梨俱吠陀》第九卷第九讚第七頌（Rv. IX. 9, 7）。
吠陀時代。107 Yag，祭祀。
梵文中獻祭的通常用詞是 YAG，粟特語 yaz，由此衍生出 yajña（獻祭）、yag-us（獻祭公式）、yájāmi（我獻祭）、yájya（應被崇拜的）。這個 yájya 曾被拿來與希臘文 ἁγιός（聖的）相比較，雖然這並不確定^。為何 yaj 應該取得獻祭或奉獻給眾神的這個含義，我們無法說明^，因為不可能將這個根詞追溯回任何更具一般性含義的其他根詞。
胡（Hu），獻祭。

另一個梵文字根經常需要用「祭祀」來翻譯，那就是 HU。在這個例子中，我們可以清楚地看到這個字根的原初意圖。它本來的意思是「傾倒」，主要用於將大麥、油和其他物質投入火中的行為。後來它取得了更為廣泛的意義，但仍不廣泛到足以適用於動物祭祀。由此衍生的梵文詞彙有 havis、havya（祭祀）、a-hava（一種罐子）、gruhu（湯匙）、hotṛ（祭司）、homa 與 ahuti（奠祭）。在希臘文中，χύ或 χεῖ 僅是「傾倒」之意，χύτρα 是陶土罐皿，θύειν 意為「祭祀」，在音韻上可能。希臘文的 ἄγος或 ἀγός 並非意指祭祀，而是淨化或贖罪。它不能是梵文 agas，因為在希臘文中 a 是短母音。
梵文神學家將 yaj（祭祀）與 iyaj（捨棄、遺棄）相連結，但此說並無類比依據。比較語言學家向來將 bhaj（禮敬）與 yaj（祭祀）並列，認為 bh（bhī）與 y（nī 或 tī）具有前置詞來源，然而此說純屬假說，早已被棄用。
3 阿爾比魯尼（Al-Biruni），第二卷，第96頁。

^ Aufrecht in Kuhn's Zeitschrift, xiv. p. 208. This root hu, to pour out, exists also in the Latin *fundus*, a water-jug, and in *vasa fundilia*, which Paulus, Ent. p. 89, explains rightly as derived *a fundendo*. *Futilis*, in the sense of futile, may have been conceived either as a man who always pours forth, or as a vessel, leaky, not holding 108 LECTURE V. --- ^ Aufrecht在Kuhn's《時代誌》第十四卷第208頁。此根詞hu（倒出、傾瀉），亦存在於拉丁文*fundus*（水罐），及*vasa fundilia*中；Paulus在《詞源學》第89頁中正確地將其解釋為源自*a fundendo*。*Futilis*（徒勞的、無益的之意），可被理解為要麼是一個經常傾瀉的人，要麼是一個漏水的容器，無法盛水 第108頁 講座五。
可以溯源至同一源頭，但其含義造成困難。
祭祀術語
